is a shallow natural rectangular [bay](/wiki/Bay "Bay") and multiple [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") on the east coast of [England](/wiki/England "England") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). It is an inlet of the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") and is the largest multiple estuary system in the UK, as well as being the largest natural bay in England, and is the outflow for the rivers [Witham](/wiki/River_Witham "River Witham"), [Welland](/wiki/River_Welland "River Welland"), [Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene") and the [Great Ouse](/wiki/River_Great_Ouse "River Great Ouse"). It is also one of the most important conservation areas in Europe, with several nature reserves within this area.

**The Wash** is a shallow natural rectangular [bay](/wiki/Bay "Bay") and multiple [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") on the east coast of [England](/wiki/England "England") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). It is an inlet of the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") and is the largest multiple estuary system in the UK, as well as being the largest natural bay in England, and is the outflow for the rivers [Witham](/wiki/River_Witham "River Witham"), [Welland](/wiki/River_Welland "River Welland"), [Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene") and the [Great Ouse](/wiki/River_Great_Ouse "River Great Ouse"). It is also one of the most important conservation areas in Europe, with several nature reserves within this area.

The coastline is partly in [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") and partly in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk,_England "Norfolk, England"). The Lincolnshire side forms part of the only coastline of the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") region, whilst the [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") side forms the north-west corner of the [East Anglian](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") region. The coastline stretches from [Gibraltar Point](/wiki/Gibraltar_Point,_Lincolnshire "Gibraltar Point, Lincolnshire") just south of the seaside town of [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness") to Gore Point near the village of [Holme-next-the-Sea](/wiki/Holme-next-the-Sea "Holme-next-the-Sea"), just east of the seaside town of [Hunstanton](/wiki/Hunstanton "Hunstanton") in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"). These two points are over 75 miles (121 km) from each other by road, but only eleven and a half miles (18.5 km) by sea.

The bay comprises multiple estuaries, marshland, deep-water channels (particularly the Boston and Lynn Deeps), and shifting shallow-water channels, all surrounded by sandbanks.

There are several large settlements near its coastline, the largest is the town of [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") in Norfolk, followed by the two slightly smaller towns of [Boston](/wiki/Boston,_Lincolnshire "Boston, Lincolnshire") in Lincolnshire and [Wisbech](/wiki/Wisbech "Wisbech") in [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"). These are the three main [inland ports](/wiki/Inland_port "Inland port") in the Wash area; a fourth smaller port is also available for shipping on the River Nene at [Sutton Bridge](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge "Sutton Bridge"), and a small marina for pleasure craft is also accessible on the tidal River Welland at [Fosdyke Bridge](/wiki/Fosdyke "Fosdyke"). Nearby, on either side of the Wash at almost opposite ends to each other, are the seaside towns of [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness") which is located on the top of the Lincolnshire side just north of [Gibraltar Point](/wiki/Gibraltar_Point,_Lincolnshire "Gibraltar Point, Lincolnshire") and [Hunstanton](/wiki/Hunstanton "Hunstanton") on the top of the Norfolk side north of the nearby royal estate of [Sandringham](/wiki/Sandringham_House "Sandringham House").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Boston_Stump_%2838810540280%29.jpg/250px-Boston_Stump_%2838810540280%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boston_Stump_(38810540280).jpg)

[Boston Stump](/wiki/St_Botolph%27s_Church,_Boston "St Botolph's Church, Boston") can be seen from virtually every part of the Wash, and locally is the oldest and most famous visible human-made landmark.

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Bentinck_Dock_at_King%27s_Lynn_-_geograph.org.uk_-_228722.jpg/250px-Bentinck_Dock_at_King%27s_Lynn_-_geograph.org.uk_-_228722.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bentinck_Dock_at_King%27s_Lynn_-_geograph.org.uk_-_228722.jpg)

The grain storage tower in [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") can also be seen from many parts of the Wash

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Sutton_Bridge_Power_Station_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4359808.jpg/250px-Sutton_Bridge_Power_Station_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4359808.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sutton_Bridge_Power_Station_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4359808.jpg)

Sutton Bridge Power Station can be seen particularly from the eastern coastline of the Wash

The Wash makes a large indentation in the coastline of [Eastern England](/wiki/Eastern_England "Eastern England") that separates Lincolnshire from the curved coast of [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"). It is a large bay with three roughly straight sides meeting at right angles, each about 15 miles (25 kilometres) in length.

The western coast, which is roughly parallel to the east coast, runs from [Gibraltar Point](/wiki/Gibraltar_Point,_Lincolnshire "Gibraltar Point, Lincolnshire") to the mouth of the River Welland just north east of the village of [Fosdyke](/wiki/Fosdyke "Fosdyke"), and is entirely within Lincolnshire. The southern coast from Fosdyke to King's Lynn runs roughly north-west to south-east, connecting these two river mouths, and is punctuated by the mouth of a third river, the River Nene, which flows into the Wash just north of [Sutton Bridge](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge "Sutton Bridge") with the county boundary between Lincolnshire and Norfolk (which is also the regional boundary between the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands "East Midlands") and the [East of England](/wiki/East_of_England "East of England")) meeting just beyond this point before it continues eastwards to Ongar Hill where it meets the mouth of the [River Great Ouse](/wiki/River_Great_Ouse "River Great Ouse").

The eastern coast of the Wash is entirely within Norfolk, and initially extends from the mouth of the River Great Ouse just north of the town of King's Lynn north eastwards towards the small village of [Wolferton](/wiki/Wolferton "Wolferton") close to the [Sandringham estate](/wiki/Sandringham_House "Sandringham House") before heading northwards from [Snettisham](/wiki/Snettisham "Snettisham") to the low lands of [Heacham](/wiki/Heacham "Heacham"), the town of Hunstanton, and the village of [Old Hunstanton](/wiki/Old_Hunstanton "Old Hunstanton"), before reaching its northern extremity at Gore Point near [Holme-next-the-Sea](/wiki/Holme-next-the-Sea "Holme-next-the-Sea"), where the Norfolk coast turns eastwards.

Inland from the Wash, the land is flat, low-lying and often marshy: these are [the Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens") of Lincolnshire, [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire") and Norfolk.

Deposition of sediment and [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") have markedly altered the coastline of the Wash in historical times. Several towns once on the coast of the Wash (notably King's Lynn) are now some distance inland. Much of the Wash itself is very shallow, with several large sandbanks, such as Breast Sand, Bulldog Sand, Roger Sand, and Old South Sand, which are exposed at low tide, especially along the south coast. These form hazards to navigation.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Three commercial shipping lane channels lead inland from the Wash:

* the [River Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene") leading to [Port Sutton Bridge](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge#Dock "Sutton Bridge") in Lincolnshire and further inland to the [Port of Wisbech](/wiki/Port_of_Wisbech "Port of Wisbech") in Cambridgeshire,
* the River Great Ouse leading to [King's Lynn Docks](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_Docks "King's Lynn Docks") in Norfolk,

both via the large channel known as the Lynn Deeps.

* The third shipping lane can be accessed via the narrower Boston Deeps channel to lead inland, via [The Haven](/wiki/The_Haven,_Boston "The Haven, Boston") (River Witham) to the [Port of Boston](/wiki/Boston,_Lincolnshire "Boston, Lincolnshire").

Each of the three shipping lanes has [maritime pilot](/wiki/Maritime_pilot "Maritime pilot") stations to guide and navigate ships carrying cargo. Large boats can now only access the [River Welland](/wiki/River_Welland "River Welland") as far as [Fosdyke Bridge](/wiki/Fosdyke "Fosdyke") since the replacement of the old swing bridge which carries the [A17 road](/wiki/A17_road_(England) "A17 road (England)") over the river with a fixed bridge in 1990 following the decline of [Spalding](/wiki/Spalding,_Lincolnshire "Spalding, Lincolnshire") as a port during the first part of the 20th century leaving only very small craft being able to use this route.

A re-survey of the coastline of the Wash carried out by the Ordnance Survey in 2011 revealed that an estimated additional 3,000 acres (1,200 hectares) on its coastline had been created by [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(geology) "Accretion (geology)") since previous surveys between 1960 and 1980.

## Water temperature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Water temperature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Ruth_Do328_Wash_16.08.12R_edited-2.jpg/250px-Ruth_Do328_Wash_16.08.12R_edited-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruth_Do328_Wash_16.08.12R_edited-2.jpg)

The Wash looking WSW from over Hunstanton. The Great Ouse and Nene are visible running south.

The Wash varies enormously in water temperature throughout the year. Winter temperatures are brought near freezing by the cold North Sea flows. Summer water temperatures can reach 20–23 °C (68–73 °F) after prolonged high ambient air temperature and sun. This effect, which typically happens in the shallow areas around beaches and often only in pockets of water, is exaggerated by the extensive sheltered [tidal reach](/wiki/Tidal_reach "Tidal reach").

## Wildlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Wildlife")]

The Wash is made up of extensive [salt marshes](/wiki/Salt_marsh "Salt marsh"), major inter-tidal banks of sand and mud, shallow waters, and deep channels. As understanding of the importance of the natural marshes has increased in the 21st century, the seawall at [Freiston](/wiki/Freiston "Freiston") has been breached in three places to increase the salt-marsh area and provide extra [habitat](/wiki/Habitat_(ecology) "Habitat (ecology)") for [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird"), particularly [waders](/wiki/Wader "Wader"), and as a natural flood-prevention measure. The extensive creeks in the salt marsh and the vegetation that grows there help dissipate wave energy, thereby enhancing the protection afforded to the land behind the salt marsh. This is an example of recent exploration into the possibilities of [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainable "Sustainable") [coastal management](/wiki/Coastal_management "Coastal management") through [soft engineering](/wiki/Soft_engineering "Soft engineering") techniques, rather than through dykes and drainage. The same scheme includes a new [brackish](/wiki/Brackish_water "Brackish water") [lagoon](/wiki/Lagoon "Lagoon") habitat.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Snettisham-jetty.JPG/250px-Snettisham-jetty.JPG)](/wiki/File:Snettisham-jetty.JPG)

The jetty at [Snettisham RSPB reserve](/wiki/Snettisham_RSPB_reserve "Snettisham RSPB reserve")

On the eastern side of the Wash, low chalk cliffs with a noted red-chalk stratum are found at Hunstanton. The [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel") pits (lagoons) found at [Snettisham RSPB reserve](/wiki/Snettisham_RSPB_reserve "Snettisham RSPB reserve") are an important roost for waders at high tide. This Special Protection Area (SPA) borders the North Norfolk Coast Special Protection Area. To the north-west, the Wash extends to Gibraltar Point, another SPA.

The partly confined nature of the Wash habitats, combined with ample tidal flows, allows [shellfish](/wiki/Shellfish "Shellfish") to breed, especially [shrimp](/wiki/Shrimp "Shrimp"), [cockles](/wiki/Cockle_(bivalve) "Cockle (bivalve)") and [mussels](/wiki/Mussel "Mussel"). Some water birds, such as [oystercatchers](/wiki/Common_pied_oystercatcher "Common pied oystercatcher"), feed on shellfish. It is also a breeding area for [common tern](/wiki/Common_tern "Common tern"), and a feeding area for [marsh harriers](/wiki/Western_marsh_harrier "Western marsh harrier"). Migrating birds such as [geese](/wiki/Goose "Goose"), [duck](/wiki/Duck "Duck"), and wading birds come to the Wash in large numbers to spend the winter, with an average total of around 400,000 birds present at any one time.[[2]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-2) It has been estimated that some two million birds a year use the Wash for feeding and roosting during their annual migrations.

The Wash is recognised as internationally important for 17 bird species. They include [pink-footed goose](/wiki/Pink-footed_goose "Pink-footed goose"), dark-bellied [brent goose](/wiki/Brent_goose "Brent goose"), [shelduck](/wiki/Common_shelduck "Common shelduck"), [pintail](/wiki/Northern_pintail "Northern pintail"), [oystercatcher](/wiki/Eurasian_oystercatcher "Eurasian oystercatcher"), [ringed plover](/wiki/Ringed_plover "Ringed plover"), [grey plover](/wiki/Grey_plover "Grey plover"), [golden plover](/wiki/Eurasian_golden_plover "Eurasian golden plover"), [lapwing](/wiki/Northern_lapwing "Northern lapwing"), [knot](/wiki/Red_knot "Red knot"), [sanderling](/wiki/Sanderling "Sanderling"), [dunlin](/wiki/Dunlin "Dunlin"), [black-tailed godwit](/wiki/Black-tailed_godwit "Black-tailed godwit"), [bar-tailed godwit](/wiki/Bar-tailed_godwit "Bar-tailed godwit"), [curlew](/wiki/Eurasian_curlew "Eurasian curlew"), [redshank](/wiki/Common_redshank "Common redshank") and [turnstone](/wiki/Ruddy_turnstone "Ruddy turnstone").[[2]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-2)

### Wash River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Wash River")]

At the end of the [latest glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_glaciation "Wisconsin glaciation"), and while the sea level remained lower than it is today, the rivers Witham, Welland, [Glen](/wiki/River_Glen,_Lincolnshire "River Glen, Lincolnshire"), Nene, and Great Ouse joined into a large river.

The deep valley of the Wash was formed, not by an interglacial river, but by ice of the [Wolstonian](/wiki/Wolstonian_Stage "Wolstonian Stage") and [Devensian](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") stages flowing southwards up the slope represented by the modern coast and forming [tunnel valleys](/wiki/Tunnel_valley "Tunnel valley"), of which the [Silver Pit](/wiki/Silver_Pit "Silver Pit") is one of many. This process gave the Silver Pit its depth and narrowness. When the tunnel valley was free of ice and seawater, it was occupied by the river. This kept it free of sediment, unlike most tunnel valleys. Since the sea flooded it, the valley seems to have been kept open by tidal action. During the [Ipswichian Stage](/wiki/Eemian_Stage "Eemian Stage"), the Wash River probably flowed by way of the site of the Silver Pit, but the tunnel valley would not have been formed at this stage, as its alignment seems inconsistent.

In [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), embankments were built along the margins of the Wash to protect agricultural land from flooding. However, they fell into disrepair after the Roman withdrawal in 407 CE.

From about 865 to about 1066, the Wash was used by the [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") as a major route for invading [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") and the [Middle England](/wiki/English_Midlands "English Midlands"). [Danes](/wiki/Danes "Danes") established themselves in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") in 875. Before the 12th century, when drainage and embankment efforts led by monks began to separate the land from the estuarine mudflats, the Wash was a tidal part of [the Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens") that reached as far as [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") and [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough "Peterborough").

Local people put up fierce resistance against the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") for some time after the 1066 [Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest_of_England "Norman Conquest of England").

The name Wash may have been derived from [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *wāse* meaning mud, slime, or ooze. The word *Wasche* is mentioned in the popular dictionary *[Promptorium parvulorum](/wiki/Promptorium_parvulorum "Promptorium parvulorum")* of about 1440 as a water or a [ford](/wiki/Ford_(crossing) "Ford (crossing)") (*vadum*). A chronicle states that [King Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI_of_England "Edward VI of England") passed *the Wasshes* as he visited the town of [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") in 1548. By then, documents began to refer to the *Waashe* or *Wysche*, but only for the tidal sands and shoals of the rivers Welland and Nene. Sixteenth-century scholars identified the Wash as the *Æstuarium Metuonis* ("The Reaping/Mowing/Cutting-Off Estuary") mentioned by [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") in Roman times. They claimed the word was still in occasional use. [William Camden](/wiki/William_Camden "William Camden") characterized *The Washes* as "a very large arme" of the "German Ocean" (the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea")), "at every tide and high sea covered all with water, but when the sea ebbeth, and the tide is past, a man may pass over it as on dry land, but yet not without danger", as [King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") learned not without his loss (see below). Inspired by Camden's account, [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") mentioned the *Lincolne-Washes* in his stage play *King John* (1616). During the 17th and 18th centuries, the name Wash came to be used for the estuary itself.

Drainage and reclamation works around the Wash continued until the 1970s. Large areas of salt marsh were progressively enclosed by banks and converted to agricultural land. The Wash is now surrounded by artificial sea defences on all three landward sides. In the 1970s, two large circular banks were built in the Terrington Marsh area of the Wash, as part of an abortive attempt to turn the entire estuary into a freshwater reservoir. The plan failed, not least because the banks were built using mud dredged from the salt marsh, which salinated the fresh water stored there.

### Hanseatic League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hanseatic League")]

From the 13th century, the market town and seaport of [Bishop's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") became the first member trading depot (*[Kontor](/wiki/Kontor "Kontor")*) in the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") of the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") of ports. During the 14th century, Lynn was the most important port in England when the League dominated sea trade with Europe. It still retains two medieval Hanseatic League warehouses: Hanse House, built in 1475, and Marriott's Warehouse.

### King John's lost baggage at the Wash (1216)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: King John's lost baggage at the Wash (1216)")]

King [John of England](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") is reported to have lost part of his [baggage train](/wiki/Baggage_train "Baggage train") at the Wash in 1216.[[3]](#cite_note-3) According to contemporary accounts, the king travelled from [Spalding](/wiki/Spalding,_Lincolnshire "Spalding, Lincolnshire"), Lincolnshire, to [Bishop's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn"), Norfolk, but fell ill and opted to return. While John took a longer route via [Wisbech](/wiki/Wisbech "Wisbech"), his baggage train—comprising horse-drawn wagons—was sent across the mouth of the Wellstream, a passage navigable only at low tide. The convoy moved too slowly to avoid the incoming tide, resulting in the loss of several wagons.

However, researcher Alan Marshall, using data from the [Royal Greenwich Observatory](/wiki/Royal_Greenwich_Observatory "Royal Greenwich Observatory") and by the [Bidston Observatory](/wiki/Bidston_Hill#Buildings "Bidston Hill") in [Birkenhead](/wiki/Birkenhead "Birkenhead"), contended that the tide had in fact been retreating. Through analysis of the reclaimed landscape, Marshall proposed that a reverse tidal surge—where the ebbing tide accelerated unexpectedly—was responsible for the incident.[[4]](#cite_note-WalkerPeterborough-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Scholars disagree on whether the royal regalia were among the baggage lost in the event.[[6]](#cite_note-Steane2003-6) Some evidence indicates that the king’s ceremonial items remained intact after the journey.[[7]](#cite_note-Twining1960-7) A later tradition suggests that John may have deposited his jewels in Lynn as security for a loan and subsequently arranged for their "loss". However, this theory is generally regarded as apocryphal.

The loss is said to have occurred near [Sutton Bridge](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge "Sutton Bridge") on the [River Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene"). John was recorded as staying the night of 12–13 October 1216 at [Swineshead Abbey](/wiki/Swineshead_Abbey "Swineshead Abbey") before continuing to [Newark-on-Trent](/wiki/Newark-on-Trent "Newark-on-Trent"), where he died of illness on 19 October.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### River and town names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: River and town names")]

The name of the river changed as a result of the redirection of the [Great Ouse](/wiki/River_Great_Ouse "River Great Ouse") in the 17th century.

Bishop's Lynn was renamed as King's Lynn in the 16th century as a result of King [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England")'s establishment of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").

## Air weapons training range

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Air weapons training range")]

A [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") [Air Weapons Range](/wiki/Bombing_range "Bombing range") (AWR) Danger Area, supporting academic and operational exercises, lies along part of the Wash coastline. This designated zone is used by the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), [Army Air Corps](/wiki/Army_Air_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "Army Air Corps (United Kingdom)"), and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") allies for bombing and air weapons training. [RAF Holbeach](/wiki/RAF_Holbeach "RAF Holbeach"), active since 1926, was originally part of the former [RAF Sutton Bridge](/wiki/RAF_Sutton_Bridge "RAF Sutton Bridge") station.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Another range situated on the Wash—[RAF Wainfleet](/wiki/RAF_Wainfleet "RAF Wainfleet")—was active from 1938 until its decommissioning in 2010.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Local tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Local tradition")]

Sailing from out of the South Lincolnshire Fens into the Wash, especially for shell-fishing, is traditionally known locally as "going down below". The origin of the phrase is unclear.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Water channels etc

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Water channels etc")]

The bay comprises multiple estuaries, marshland, deep-water channels (in particular, the Boston and Lynn Deeps), and shifting shallow-water channels, all surrounded by sandbanks. Due to these features, it is considered one of the most challenging and hazardous places for sea navigation in the world. It is fed by the rivers [Witham](/wiki/River_Witham "River Witham"), [Welland](/wiki/River_Welland "River Welland"), [Nene](/wiki/River_Nene "River Nene") and [Great Ouse](/wiki/Great_Ouse "Great Ouse"). It is a 620 km2 (240 sq. mi.) biological [Site of Special Scientific Interest](/wiki/Site_of_Special_Scientific_Interest "Site of Special Scientific Interest").[[13]](#cite_note-dsv-13)[[14]](#cite_note-map-14) It is also a [Nature Conservation Review](/wiki/Nature_Conservation_Review "Nature Conservation Review") site, Grade I,[[15]](#cite_note-15) a [National Nature Reserve](/wiki/National_nature_reserve_(United_Kingdom) "National nature reserve (United Kingdom)"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) a [Ramsar site](/wiki/Ramsar_site "Ramsar site"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) a [Special Area of Conservation](/wiki/Special_Area_of_Conservation "Special Area of Conservation")[[18]](#cite_note-18) and a [Special Protection Area](/wiki/Special_Protection_Area "Special Protection Area").[[19]](#cite_note-19) It is in the [Norfolk Coast](/wiki/Norfolk_Coast_AONB "Norfolk Coast AONB") [Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty")[[20]](#cite_note-20) and part of it is the Snettisham [Royal Society for the Protection of Birds](/wiki/Royal_Society_for_the_Protection_of_Birds "Royal Society for the Protection of Birds") nature reserve.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

The four most distinctive artificial landmarks that can be seen from the bay are:

* [St Botolph's Church](/wiki/St_Botolph%27s_Church,_Boston "St Botolph's Church, Boston") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston,_Lincolnshire "Boston, Lincolnshire"), the parish church of Boston is the most famous landmark to be seen in the bay. It has a tower known as the [Boston Stump](/wiki/Boston_Stump "Boston Stump"). This can be seen on clear days right across the bay and from the Norfolk side of the Wash, particularly from [Hunstanton](/wiki/Hunstanton "Hunstanton"). This dates from the 16th century and was often used by local fishermen as a navigation marker.
* The Grain Storage Tower in [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn") docks.
* From the Norfolk side of the bay, [Sutton Bridge Power Station](/wiki/Sutton_Bridge_Power_Station "Sutton Bridge Power Station")
* The former lighthouse at [Old Hunstanton](/wiki/Old_Hunstanton "Old Hunstanton") can also be seen from parts of the bay from the Lincolnshire side of the bay on clear days, particularly from [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness,_Lincolnshire "Skegness, Lincolnshire") and also from [Gibraltar Point](/wiki/Gibraltar_Point,_Lincolnshire "Gibraltar Point, Lincolnshire").

The [Outer Trial Bank](/wiki/Outer_Trial_Bank "Outer Trial Bank"), a remnant of a 1970s experiment, lies some two miles (three kilometres) off the Lincolnshire coast near the mouth of the River Nene.

The most notable natural feature is [Hunstanton Cliffs](/wiki/Hunstanton_Cliffs "Hunstanton Cliffs"), comprising [strata](/wiki/Strata "Strata") of orange, red, and white [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk").

## Proposed racetrack

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wash&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Proposed racetrack")]

In 1934, a proposal was made, supported by racing driver [Malcolm Campbell](/wiki/Malcolm_Campbell "Malcolm Campbell"), to build a 15-mile-long (24-kilometre) race track on reclaimed land from Boston to [Gibraltar Point](/wiki/Gibraltar_Point,_Lincolnshire "Gibraltar Point, Lincolnshire"), near [Skegness](/wiki/Skegness "Skegness"). It would have been used as a road to Skegness when there was no racing. There was also to be a long lake for boat racing inside the track loop. The financial straits in the 1930s prevented the project from proceeding.
